generated_summary: A campaign mailer sent by Republican state Senate candidate Ed Charamut in Connecticut has been widely criticized for using anti-Semitic tropes in an illustration of his Democratic challenger, Matthew Lesser, who is Jewish. The advertisement depicts Lesser holding a wad of cash with a crazed look in his eyes and was sent out by Charamut's campaign. The mailer has been denounced as offensive by Jewish groups and has drawn widespread outrage due to concerns about rising anti-Semitism, particularly in the wake of the massacre of 11 Jewish congregants at a Pittsburgh synagogue. Charamut initially defended his use of the image, claiming it was intended to be a comment on tax policy, but later issued an apology and acknowledged that the imagery could be interpreted as anti-Semitic. The state Republican Party has also acknowledged the mailer's anti-Semitic undertones and plans to meet with the Anti-Defamation League to discuss the issue.

Greenpeace activists scaled London's Shard, Europe's tallest skyscraper, as a protest against oil drilling in the Arctic by Shell. Six women from different countries climbed the 72-storey building over a period of 16 hours, reaching the top at around 7:10 PM. The activists were arrested on suspicion of aggravated trespass and their climb was live-streamed using helmet cameras. The Shard was chosen for the protest as it is visible from Shell's three London offices and because its design resembles a shard of ice. Shell released a statement saying they respect the right to free expression, but also emphasized that oil and gas production in the Arctic is not new and can help maintain energy security. The climb caused the closure of the Shard's viewing platform for safety reasons, but offices and restaurants remained open.

Sudanese President Omar al-Bashir, who is wanted for war crimes and genocide, left South Africa ahead of a court ruling that would have ordered his arrest. The South African High Court had ruled on Sunday that al-Bashir should remain in the country while it considered whether he should be arrested, but lawyers argued that ports were not obeying the judge's order. Al-Bashir's departure came as the court was considering a request by the International Criminal Court (ICC) to arrest him on charges of war crimes, crimes against humanity, and genocide stemming from the conflict in Darfur. The ICC has accused al-Bashir of ordering military, police, and Janjaweed militia attacks on ethnic groups deemed sympathetic to rebel outfits with the intent to destroy them. South Africa's ruling party accused the ICC of being biased against Africans and "no longer useful." The ICC expressed disappointment at al-Bashir's ability to escape, but deputy prosecutor James Stewart said it was not a setback for the court as they were playing "a long game." The U.S. State Department also expressed disappointment that South Africa did not prevent al-Bashir from leaving Johannesburg.

The Budweiser "Brotherhood" ad featuring a man raising and reuniting with a Clydesdale horse is now the third most-shared Super Bowl ad of all time, with over 1.5 million shares on Facebook. The ad, set to Fleetwood Mac's "Landslide," showcases tear-jerking scenes of the horse's first steps, the owner sleeping in the stable, and the horse running alongside his moving car. Budweiser's first tweet asked fans to name the foal, which was born on Jan 16.

The Super Bowl ads this year have been generally disappointing, with Dodge's ad featuring a talk by Paul Harvey, "So God Made a Farmer," being the most striking. The Millennium Tower in San Francisco, once celebrated as the tallest residential building west of the Mississippi, is facing significant issues due to sinking and tilting. Despite its opulence and high-profile residents like former 49ers quarterback Joe Montana, the tower has developed cracks and is sinking by 17 inches and tilting 14 inches towards neighboring buildings. The building's developers, Millennium Partners, blame construction of the Transbay Terminal next door for the instability, while residents and some engineers argue that the foundation was not built to bedrock, as is necessary for a concrete building of its size. The tower has spawned numerous lawsuits, and some homeowners have already sold their apartments at a loss due to concerns about the building's safety. Engineers are currently exploring potential fixes, such as drilling new piles down to bedrock, but the cost and responsibility for the repairs remain unclear. The story has captivated San Francisco and raised questions about the city's development practices.

The United States healthcare system is ranked last out of 11 industrialized nations in terms of quality, despite being the most expensive, according to a new report by the Commonwealth Fund. The U.K., which has a single-payer healthcare system, ranks first, followed by Switzerland. The report highlights several issues with the US system, including a lack of universal health coverage, difficulties receiving timely information and coordinating care, a shortage of primary care physicians, and high levels of infant mortality and mortality from preventable conditions. The Affordable Care Act (Obamacare) may improve the US ranking in future studies by providing health insurance to some of the 50 million uninsured people. However, the report suggests that significant reforms are needed to address the pervasive problems with the US healthcare system.

A 1-year-old girl named Katera Barker died after being accidentally left in a hot car parked at her home in Nashville, Tennessee. Her father, Matt Barker, dropped off his children at day care but forgot about Katera. He then returned home and left for the airport on a business trip, while his wife went to work thinking both children were at day care. Katera's mother discovered she was still in the car when she went to pick up the children from day care that evening. The tragedy marks the seventh child death due to being left in a hot car this year, and more than half of such deaths result from caregivers forgetting that the child is in the vehicle. A memory aid to help parents avoid such tragedies is placing an item like a purse or shoe worn in the back seat with the child. Tennessee law allows individuals to pull children from locked cars if harm is imminent and no charges have been filed in this case. The high temperature on the day of the incident reached 89 degrees, which could have caused the temperature inside the car to reach nearly 120 degrees.

A man on United Airlines Flight 116 from Hong Kong to the U.S., identified as Daniel Morgan Perry, caused a disturbance during the flight by ranting about national security, the CIA, and international spying. He was reportedly afraid of being poisoned by fellow passengers and demanded that the plane be diverted to Canada. Passengers restrained him, and he was later hospitalized for observation at Newark's University Hospital. The flight landed at Newark Liberty International Airport, where the FBI met the plane and took the man into custody. He allegedly claimed to have information about Edward Snowden, a former U.S. government contractor who leaked classified NSA surveillance program details two weeks prior. The incident caused minor delays for other passengers on the flight.

The study "Brainprint" by researchers from Binghamton University suggests using brainwaves as an alternative to traditional passwords for security verification. By observing and recording brain signals in volunteers while they read a list of acronyms, the researchers found that each participant's brain reacted differently to each acronym, allowing a computer system to identify each volunteer with 94% accuracy. This method could potentially offer advantages over other biometrics like fingerprints or retinas, as brain biometrics are cancellable and cannot be stolen by malicious means. The researchers see this technology having potential in high-security environments such as military facilities, but not for low-security applications in the near future.

The South Carolina massacre suspect, Dylann Roof, has been arrested for allegedly shooting and killing nine people at a historic African-American church in South Carolina on Wednesday. Those who know him described him as a withdrawn, troubled young man. His uncle recalled that he was too introverted, had no job or driver's license, and spent most of his time in his room. Roof's uncle also mentioned that for his birthday this year, his father bought him a pistol.

In a separate incident, the Ferguson police officer who shot Michael Brown, an unarmed black teenager, was identified as Darren Wilson. The shooting occurred after Brown was suspected of being in the road and not during a convenience store robbery, despite the release of surveillance video showing Brown pushing aside a smaller man and leaving the store with a box of cigars.

The Ferguson police chief expressed "every confidence" in Officer Wilson, but many remain upset that additional details have not been released about Brown's shooting. The information released has fueled anger and distrust towards the Ferguson police department. Protests demanding action by authorities are set to continue, with a major rally scheduled for Sunday.

The first archaeological excavations at the Nazi death camp Treblinka have revealed new mass graves, gas chambers, and physical evidence of the atrocities committed there. These findings are presented in the documentary "Treblinka: Hitler's Killing Machine," which airs on the Smithsonian Channel on March 29. The excavations were led by forensic archaeologist Dr. Caroline Sturdy Colls, who discovered that the Nazis weren't as successful at covering up their crimes as they believed when they razed the death camp in 1943. Brick walls and foundations from the gas chambers remain, along with massive amounts of human bone. The documentary also reveals details about Treblinka's horrific history, including the elaborate deception used to send Jews to their deaths, the role of Jewish violinist Artur Gold in entertaining the Nazi guards, and the brutal treatment of prisoners in both the labor and death camps. The excavations provide a deeper understanding of the atrocities committed at Treblinka and serve as a reminder of the importance of documenting evil with concrete certainty.

The towns affected by the quake were described as being reduced to rubble, with entire blocks of buildings turned into piles of sand and rock. Residents emerged from their crumbled homes to find apocalyptic scenes, with thick dust choking the air and a putrid smell of gas. British police are planning to travel to Portugal to oversee excavations in the Algarve resort where Madeleine McCann, who went missing seven years ago, was last seen. Conard argues that the crisis was not caused by corrupt bankers selling dodgy financial products, but rather by a simple run on the banks. The article discusses the impact of the marijuana industry on the commercial real estate market in Denver, Colorado. According to a report by CBRE, one in eleven industrial buildings in central Denver is now used for marijuana cultivation, occupying about 3.7 million square feet of space. This has significantly influenced the market, leading to near-record high rents, low vacancy rates, and tenants struggling to find space. Marijuana tenants often pay a premium of two to three times the average lease rate. The industry's impact on the real estate market is expected to continue as traditional industrial demand recovers from the Great Recession. Seaman James Derek Lovelace, a 21-year-old Navy SEAL trainee, died during his first week of basic training at Naval Amphibious Base Coronado in California on Friday. The cause of death is currently under investigation by the Navy, and an autopsy will be conducted by local authorities. Lovelace was participating in a swimming exercise known as "drown-proofing" when he experienced difficulty and lost consciousness. Despite resuscitation efforts on-scene and at a nearby hospital, he was pronounced dead.

Lovelace was born in Germany and attended Crestview High School in Florida before studying mechanical engineering at Faulkner State Community College in Alabama. He enlisted in the Navy six months prior to his death and had already graduated from boot camp. Lovelace is survived by his father, two sisters, and numerous family members.

The Navy has expressed its condolences to Lovelace's family and friends and will conduct an investigation into his death. This incident follows the recent death of another Coronado-based SEAL, Navy Petty Officer 1st Class Charles Keating IV, who was killed in a gunbattle involving Islamic State fighters in Iraq.

A lawsuit was filed by an Army wife, Amy Herbst, against the government on Monday, alleging that a botched childbirth operation at Blanchfield Army Community Hospital at Fort Campbell, Ky., in 2012 has jeopardized her opera career. The lawsuit claims that a nurse-midwife performed an episiotomy during the second stage of Herbst’s labor without consent or knowledge, causing significant damage to her digestive and reproductive system. As a result, Herbst has experienced fecal urgency, incontinence, and requires reconstructive surgery, which has prevented her from continuing her career as a professional opera singer at the Nashville Opera Company. The hospital spokesperson declined to comment on the specific case but stated that they strive to provide high-quality healthcare. Meanwhile, US troops and civilians were reportedly killed in a suicide attack in Syria.

The White House has partially disclosed its counterterrorism campaign against al-Qaida in Yemen and Somalia by admitting for the first time that it conducts lethal attacks in these countries. The semiannual report to Congress on U.S. combat operations abroad, delivered Friday, mentions the U.S. military's "direct action" against members of al-Qaida and affiliates in Yemen and Somalia. This term refers to a range of lethal attacks, including drone strikes, but the report does not mention drones specifically. The report only applies to U.S. military operations, not those conducted by the CIA. President Obama's decision to declassify the existence of these counterterror actions in Yemen and Somalia represents an incremental move towards greater openness about U.S. force usage overseas. This announcement does not reflect any change in the intensity or basic character of the U.S. campaign against al-Qaida. The report acknowledges limited military operations in both countries but does not provide details. The disclosure comes amid outcries from some in Congress about leaks to the news media about classified activities, including a White House "kill list" of targeted al-Qaida militants. In Arlington, Virginia, a man named Anthony Jones was found living in the attic of a house after the tenant heard noises on July 21. Police were alerted by the landlord and arrested Jones, charging him with unlawful entry. The incident is believed to have occurred through an open sliding glass door. This report also includes other crime incidents in Arlington County for the same period, such as robbery, larceny from auto, shoplifting, destruction of property, and a stolen vehicle.

In December 1963, two women from Essex, Barbara Bezant and Lyn Jeffries, sent a reel-to-reel tape with a message for The Beatles during their tour. Punjab Chief Minister Shahbaz Sharif has ordered a three-member committee to investigate the killings and increased security at Christian neighborhoods in the province. Blasphemy is a sensitive issue in Pakistan, and even unproven allegations can lead to mob violence or violent deaths. A Christian woman, Aasia Bibi, has been on death row since 2010 for making derogatory remarks about Prophet Muhammad, and an elderly British man sentenced to death for blasphemy was shot by a prison guard last month. The blasphemy law disproportionately impacts minorities, with entire communities suffering when one member is accused. The desire to grab land or settle personal scores often underlies blasphemy allegations, and the law needs to be revisited. This potential re-engagement was discussed during a climate change meeting in Montreal led by Canada, China, and the European Union, with U.S. officials suggesting revising U.S. climate goals as a compromise.

The president's derogatory comment caused an uproar and concern among some Republican allies who are worried about the president alienating women voters in the weeks before the November midterm elections. The timing of Trump's attack on Daniels confused some of his GOP allies, who are concerned about the impact it may have on Republican candidates' ability to attract suburban women voters.

This news article discusses a collection of love letters written by John F. Kennedy to Gunilla von Post, a Swedish socialite, which were sold at auction by Doyle New York for $15,000. The letters, dated from June 1954 to August 1956, were found among von Post's belongings after her death in 2011. Kennedy and von Post met in Cannes, France, in the summer of 1953, and their affair continued even after Kennedy married Jackie Kennedy in September 1953. The letters offer a glimpse into their long-distance love affair, with Kennedy expressing his desire to meet von Post in Stockholm and later trying to arrange a rendezvous in Sweden. The letters also detail the obstacles that prevented them from being together, including Kennedy's political ambitions, his father, and Jackie's miscarriage in 1955 and pregnancy in 1956. Mitt Romney, the 2012 Republican presidential nominee, delivered a speech on March 3, 2016, criticizing Donald Trump's political vision and qualifications for the presidency. Romney accused Trump of being a "phony" and a "fraud," and labeled his promises as worthless. He also pointed out Trump's lack of foreign policy experience, bullying behavior, and misogyny.

Romney's speech came after Trump had won several primary elections and was leading in the polls. Romney did not endorse any of Trump's opponents but praised Florida Senator Marco Rubio, Texas Senator Ted Cruz, and Ohio Governor John Kasich. He also expressed concerns about a potential Hillary Clinton presidency.

Trump responded to Romney's speech with a series of tweets, accusing Romney of being soft on conservative issues such as immigration and abortion. Trump also mocked Romney's failed business ventures and sexual indiscretions.

The speech was significant because it marked a rare instance of a former Republican presidential nominee publicly criticizing the party's front-runner, and it reflected growing concerns within the Republican Party about Trump's qualifications for the presidency.

The Los Angeles Police Department is seeking public assistance in identifying three armed and dangerous bank robbers who have committed 24 robberies since November 2015. Superstorm Sandy caused significant damage across the East Coast of the United States, resulting in at least 46 deaths as of Tuesday. The storm affected 20 states from New England to Tennessee, with the hardest hit areas being New York City and New Jersey. More than 6.6 million homes and businesses were without electricity, and major transportation systems were disrupted. Subway service in New York City was unlikely to resume for four to five days, and airports in the affected regions were closed or offering limited service. The storm caused extensive damage to infrastructure, with at least four towns in north New Jersey being submerged by up to 6 feet of water after a levee broke. The cost of the destruction is estimated to be between $7 billion and $15 billion, with President Obama promising federal assistance for recovery efforts.

The article discusses a study led by Alexander Hristov of Pennsylvania State University, which found that feeding cows a substance called 3-nitrooxypropanol (3NOP) can reduce their methane emissions by approximately 30%. Methane is a potent greenhouse gas and a significant contributor to climate change. The study was published in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences.

The researchers mixed additives containing varying concentrations of 3NOP into cattle feed and administered it to 48 Holstein cows for three months. They found that the rate of methane emission fell in all animals, except for those receiving a placebo.

3NOP appears to be safe for cows' health and the environment, and its use could help improve the efficiency of feed by reducing the amount of energy lost as methane. The researchers plan to conduct further studies to investigate how 3NOP affects a cow's gut bacteria.

The study offers hope that reducing methane emissions from livestock could help mitigate climate change. Agriculture is a significant source of methane in the U.S., and policymakers may need to take a multifaceted approach to reduce net methane emissions.

Bannon has also expressed interest in preventing Mitt Romney from running for the Senate in 2018 and may endorse incumbent Utah senator Orrin Hatch, who has been in politics since 1976.

In France, there has been an unexplained cluster of babies born without hands or parts of their forearms in three rural areas between 2007 and 2017. The cases were first reported near the village of Druillat in the Ain department, where seven babies were born without arms or hands between 2009 and 2014. Three years later, similar cases were flagged around Mouzeil in the Loire-Atlantique department and Guidel in Brittany.

The French public health authority, Santé Publique France, has admitted that the situations in Loire-Atlantique and Brittany reveal an excessive number of these kinds of cases, but they do not believe the evidence suggests there has been an excessive level of cases in Ain. However, a research organization called Remera, which looks into cases of malformation, conducted an investigation into the situation in the Ain department and dismissed genetics, as well as drugs and drink, as possible causes for the missing arms.

Remera believes that the most likely cause behind this unusual situation is the agricultural industry, meaning pesticides. This theory is bolstered by reports that several calves were born missing tails and ribs at Chalamont, another village in the Ain department, at the time the cluster of disfigured babies occurred.

New Zealand Prime Minister Jacinda Ardern has given birth to a girl, making her the second elected leader in modern history to give birth while in office. The baby was born at Auckland City Hospital on June 21st, weighing 3.31kg (7.3lb). Ardern passed on her duties to Deputy Prime Minister Winston Peters during a six-week maternity leave but will continue to be consulted on significant issues and read cabinet papers. The birth was announced on social media, with Ardern expressing gratitude for the well wishes received. Notably, Ardern arrived at the hospital in her own car, choosing a public hospital over private care. This event mirrors the 1990 maternity of former Pakistani Prime Minister Benazir Bhutto, who also gave birth while in office and kept her pregnancy private due to political instability.

The famous photograph showing General Ulysses S. Grant astride his horse at City Point, Virginia during the Civil War is actually a compilation of three separate images. The discovery was made by researchers at the Library of Congress in 2007. The image was created by L.C. Handy, a nephew of Civil War photographer Mathew Brady, who used photographs of Grant's head, Maj. Gen. Alexander M. McCook on horseback, and Confederate prisoners captured at Fisher’s Hill to create the composite photograph. The Library of Congress notes that this photograph is a montage or composite of several images and does not actually show General Ulysses S. Grant at City Point. The composite photograph violates our understanding of human nature and admonishes us to not believe everything we see, especially when it comes to historical photographs produced during the Civil War era.

This article discusses the discovery of a 2,000-year-old Roman shipwreck off the coast of Spain, which was found to be carrying a large cargo of garum, a fish sauce highly prized by the Romans. The ship, dating back to around AD 50, is one of the largest Roman shipwrecks ever discovered and contained over 1,200 well-preserved clay jars called amphoras.

The ship was likely sailing from Cadiz, Spain to Rome when it sank in a storm. The presence of an oxygenated water fish tank on board suggests that the Romans may have maintained live fish on their ships for consumption during long voyages. A lead pipe and hand-operated piston pump were found, which could have been used to fill and drain the fish tank.

The find is significant as it provides insight into ancient trade routes and Roman maritime technology. The shipwreck was discovered in 2000 and has been the subject of further investigation by marine archaeologists. However, efforts are being made to protect the site from looters.

The Energy Information Administration (EIA) reported that in March 2017, ten percent of all electricity generated in the U.S. came from wind and solar power, marking a historical first. This milestone indicates a significant increase in the use of renewable energy sources in the U.S., with annual contributions reaching about 7 percent, up from less than 1 percent a decade ago.

Texas is the country's biggest wind power producer, and California is the largest solar producer. The EIA attributes this growth to state renewables goals, infrastructure changes such as new transmission lines, and the cost-competitiveness of wind and commercial-scale solar.

Despite actions by the Trump administration to support coal energy, electric power companies are increasingly turning to less-polluting natural gas, wind, and solar power due to their lower costs. The renewable energy milestone comes amidst a growing public and corporate appetite for clean, renewable energy.

In addition, a separate study found that stationary energy batteries had a booming first quarter of 2017, with a record 234 MWh of storage installed around the U.S. This surge was partially caused by a mandate from California regulators following a massive methane leak.

The students, members of a swim team from Rancho Verde High School in Moreno Valley, encountered rough conditions and panic ensued, leading to the rescue operation. Some students suffered from hypothermia and shock, with one 16-year-old girl being taken to the hospital. On December 13, 2018, a wave of bomb threats were emailed to numerous schools, universities, businesses, and news outlets across the United States, from New York City to San Francisco. The threats demanded bitcoin payments, with many threatening to detonate explosive devices if the payment was not made.

Authorities, including the New York City Police Department and the FBI, deemed these threats as hoaxes and not credible. No actual explosive devices were discovered, but police departments across the country were forced to monitor and investigate the threats.

The emails were sent from a server in Moscow, although it's unclear whether the perpetrators were located in Russia or elsewhere in the world. The poorly worded emails used non-native English.

Many cities reported similar threats, including Chicago, Oklahoma City, Seattle, Detroit, Buffalo, Arlington and Irving in the Dallas-Fort Worth metropolitan area, Massachusetts, Orlando, and San Francisco. Schools such as Capital University in Ohio, the University of Washington, Penn State, and Niagara University also received bomb threats.

Local police departments were notified, and federal authorities were assisting in the investigation. Despite the hoax nature of the threats, they caused disruptions and evacuations at several locations.

The new study published in GeoHealth by Stony Brook University reveals that lunar dust could pose a significant health risk to astronauts spending extended periods on the Moon or other airless planetary bodies. The research shows that simulated lunar soil is toxic to human lung and mouse brain cells, with up to 90% of these cells dying when exposed to dust particles mimicking lunar soil. Prolonged exposure could lead to impaired airway and lung function, increased risk of diseases like bronchitis, and potentially cancer due to inflammation in the lungs.

Lunar dust is electrostatically charged due to constant bombardment by solar wind, which causes it to cling to spacesuits and other surfaces. During the Apollo missions, astronauts experienced symptoms similar to hay fever after inhaling lunar dust, but the new study aims to understand the long-term effects on human health. The researchers used Earth soil samples that resemble lunar highlands and volcanic plains to conduct their experiments.

The findings indicate that lunar dust could cause irritation to the throat, lungs, and eyes of future astronauts, with continued damage, irritation, and inflammation increasing the risk of more serious diseases over time. The family was taken to hospitals in Jasper and Edmonton, where Peter underwent surgery for his brain bleed and is now recovering well. Lexi had a small scratch on her face but now complains of neck pain and has nightmares. Angela still has significant pain and spends most of her time resting in a hospital bed at home due to nerve damage in her left leg and broken vertebrae.

The American Heart Association and the American College of Cardiology have released new guidelines aimed at preventing heart attacks and strokes. These guidelines focus on cholesterol-lowering statin drugs, a new way to assess risk, and a healthy lifestyle.

The updated recommendations for treating cholesterol are a significant departure from doctors' usual procedure. The new method counts strokes as well as heart attacks in its risk calculations, which will likely make some additional people candidates for the drugs. It is unclear whether more or fewer people will end up taking the drugs under the new guidelines, but many women and African-Americans may find themselves candidates for treatment due to their higher stroke risk.

The previous guidelines put a strong emphasis on lowering cholesterol levels by specific amounts, leading patients who did not hit their target levels just by taking statins to be prescribed additional drugs like Zetia. However, the new guidelines say doctors should no longer prescribe these extra medicines because they have never been shown to prevent heart attacks or strokes.

Zetia has been viewed with increasing skepticism in recent years due to studies showing it lowers LDL cholesterol but does not reduce the risk of cardiovascular disease or death. The article emphasizes the importance of consuming fruits and vegetables as part of a healthy diet, with increased intake being linked to reduced risks of noncommunicable diseases (NCDs) such as cardiovascular disease and certain types of cancer. The researchers did not know if eating even more fruits and vegetables would have greater health benefits due to limited evidence. However, they emphasized the importance of consuming whole plant foods for optimal health rather than taking antioxidant or vitamin supplements.

Sadie L. Riggs, a 15-year-old girl from Bedford, Pennsylvania, took her own life by hanging herself. According to her family's obituary posted on Facebook and the Louis Geisel Funeral Home website, Sadie was struggling with emotional issues and was in counseling while taking medication. The obituary states that she faced bullying, much of it on social media, which exacerbated her struggles.

The family believes that the bullies were effective in making Sadie feel worthless and asks people to be kind to one another instead of sending flowers. Bedford Area Superintendent Allen Sell acknowledges the difficulty of policing social media bullying as schools don't have jurisdiction outside school hours, but the district is offering counseling to students affected by Sadie's death.

The obituary also mentions that Sadie was born on Dec. 4, 2001, and is survived by her father, Eric Riggs, stepmother Alicia Smith, biological mother Beverly (Dodson) Riggs, and maternal aunt Sarah Smith, with whom she lived. A funeral service will be held at the Louis Geisel Funeral Home in Bedford on Saturday at 5 p.m.

A Utah woman named Jan Harding remains in critical condition after drinking sweet tea laced with an industrial cleaning chemical from a self-serve beverage station at Dickey's Barbecue Pit restaurant in South Jordan, Utah. The tea contained sodium hydroxide, also known as lye, which is used to clean deep fryers and can be found in drain cleaners.

According to the victim's attorney, Paxton Guymon, a worker mistakenly added the cleaning product to the iced-tea dispenser, thinking it was sugar. The incident occurred on Sunday, August 14th, and Harding has been in critical condition since then. She is unable to talk and is fighting for her life at a Salt Lake City hospital's burn unit.

South Jordan police are still investigating how the chemical ended up in the sweet-tea container, but they believe it was an accident. They have determined Harding is the only victim, as she was the first to drink the tea that day and restaurant employees dumped it out after she was burned. The restaurant remains open, and health officials are awaiting results of the criminal investigation to determine if any violations should be issued.

The company behind Dickey's Barbecue Pit has expressed sympathy for Harding and her family and is cooperating with investigators. Legal action will be determined once the police investigation is complete

Sen. John McCain has threatened to seek a subpoena to obtain information about the attack in Niger that left four US soldiers dead and two wounded. The Defense Department is conducting an initial review of the incident, but McCain, who is the chairman of the Senate armed services committee, expressed concern about the Trump administration's lack of transparency and may not wait for the department to finish its investigation before seeking details. McCain has had a "good conversation" with national security adviser H.R. McMaster and they are currently scheduling a briefing on the matter. The U.S. military is still searching for answers about what happened in Niger two weeks ago, and the Pentagon's Africa Command (AFRICOM) has sent a team to conduct a review of the facts. Some of the information being sought includes details about where the U.S. forces were when the attack occurred, whether they had adequate personal protective equipment, and if there was adequate intelligence in advance of the mission. The attack has become a political issue, with President Trump facing criticism for not initially calling the families of the fallen soldiers and for alleged content of his call to the widow of one of the four soldiers.

The 195 countries participating in the COP21 talks in Paris have agreed on a deal to combat global climate change. The accord aims to limit the increase in global average temperature to well below 2°C above pre-industrial levels, with an aspirational goal of 1.5°C. The agreement is legally binding and includes provisions for developed countries to provide financial assistance to developing countries for decarbonization efforts and adaptation to climate change impacts. Countries will also report their progress every five years, with the aim of incrementally increasing ambition across the board. The deal was met with widespread support from world leaders, although some have expressed concerns about its effectiveness in limiting warming to 1.5°C.

A Pennsylvania woman named Ella Ebaugh was awarded a record $57 million verdict against Johnson & Johnson (J&J) after suffering injuries from a vaginal mesh implant used to treat stress urinary incontinence. The implant, made by J&J's Ethicon unit, eroded into her urethra and caused her ongoing pain and complications.

Internal company emails disclosed during the trial suggest that J&J executives considered suppressing unfavorable data about the device to protect its future. The device was launched without a clinical trial and was withdrawn in 2012 after being used in thousands of operations, despite early indications of a high failure rate.

The implant is one of several vaginal mesh products that have been the subject of growing controversy due to debilitating complications experienced by some women, including severe pelvic pain, mesh erosion through the vaginal wall, and organ perforation. Class action lawsuits are underway in various countries, with lawyers claiming that patients have been exposed to unacceptable risks.

Ebaugh's case was heard in Philadelphia, and she said her complications have left her with constant pelvic pain, making it difficult for her to enjoy events like her daughter's wedding. A gunfight broke out during a wedding party in Deh Salah district, Baghlan province, northern Afghanistan, resulting in at least 21 deaths and eight injuries. The clashes erupted between two armed groups who attended the wedding, with most of the casualties being wedding guests. The cause of the dispute is unclear, but it is believed that hostility between the two groups had been simmering for years. This incident highlights the ongoing security issues in Afghanistan, where celebratory gunfire and militant attacks are common. Despite ongoing peace talks between the Afghan government and Taliban insurgents, violent incidents continue to occur, particularly in the northern provinces.

Two students from Plano East Senior High School, Ritu Sachdeva and Hillary Kate Kuizon, were found dead within hours of each other over the weekend. The District of Columbia (D.C.) is proposing a new law that would grant residents and workers 16 weeks of paid leave for medical or family reasons, making it the most generous paid leave policy in the country. If passed, the bill would offer D.C. workers the most extensive paid leave benefits, surpassing those offered by European countries where parents can take up to a year of paid time off following the birth of a child.

The Universal Paid Leave Act of 2015, introduced by council members Elissa Silverman and David Grosso, would cover almost all D.C. workers, including federal government employees who live in D.C., and residents who work outside the city, provided they opt into the system by making personal contributions to a city-managed leave fund. Employers would also contribute to the fund based on their employees' salaries.

The bill aims to address income inequality and make it easier for women to reenter the workforce. It is supported by President Obama's administration, which has spent years advocating for policies to address rising income inequality but faced partisan gridlock in Congress. The proposed measure represents a new strategy in executive branch workarounds, using local law to forge a policy bulkhead for bigger changes nationally.
